prompts.text: 'Subitise' — seeing how many there are at a glance, without counting one by one. Small groups (1–3) can often be subitised. Train the habit: 'Don't count — just look. How many can you see?'
Model the cardinality principle: when counting together, always end with '...and that means there are [N] altogether.' The last number IS the total — many children don't know this yet.
Before they answer: 'How many do you think there are — without counting? Can you just see it?' — distinguishes subitising from counting. Both are valid; name the difference.
Core comparison question: 'Is that more or fewer than before? How do you know?' — builds quantity comparison vocabulary and encourages reasoning not just answering.
